JavaScript Core Concepts (7): not defined VS undefined — What's the Difference?
are two more really important concepts in JavaScript. If you can get your head around the difference between them, debugging gets a whole lot easier.
In the earlier chapters we learned that JavaScript has this thing called hoisting (heads up — JavaScript doesn't
have hoisting, it's just a way of describing what happens so it's easier to reason about). Because of how this works, when you call a variable before it's declared, you just get

console.log(a); // a is not defined
// var a = 'Ray';
From this we can see that
means "this variable or function isn't defined."
So when you're coding and you hit
, that's your sign you forgot to declare a variable or function.
is a special keyword, and it usually shows up because of hoisting. But there's one important thing I want to add here.

literally means "empty value," and using it for an empty value is way better than reaching for
— it'll also save you from getting hauled out for a coffee chat (you know, the kind where they let you go).

is a long-standing bug in JavaScript. Because JavaScript has been running this way for so long, fixing this bug now could break websites all over the world — so it just gets to live on inside JavaScript forever.
Finally, on top of not using
as a variable's value, don't use it as a variable name either. JavaScript lets you do this, but it won't work properly.
So there are two key takeaways for
: don't use it as a variable value, and don't use it as a variable name — otherwise you'll be the one getting invited out for that coffee.

not defined and undefined are two really important concepts in JavaScript, and they trip people up all the time. Once you understand the difference between them, debugging gets a whole lot easier. This post walks through what each one actually means, and why you should never use undefined as a value or a variable name.
